mintyr 0.1.4
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Get started
  • Changelog

Export Data to XLSX Files

Source: R/export_xlsx.R
export_xlsx.Rd

The natural complement to import_xlsx(). Accepts either a combined data.frame (as produced by import_xlsx() with rbind = TRUE) or a named list of data.frames, and writes the result to disk.

The output destination is controlled by a single path argument – there are no separate modes to choose:

  • path is a directory (no .xlsx extension) – write one .xlsx file per element into that directory. Each file contains a single sheet named after the list element (or the file_col value for data.frame input).

  • path is a file (ends in .xlsx) – write everything into a single workbook, one sheet per list element (or per sheet_col / file_col value for data.frame input).

Named-list input

Pass data as a named list of data.frames:

res <- list(res1 = data1, res2 = data2, res3 = data3)

  # Directory mode -- writes res1.xlsx, res2.xlsx, res3.xlsx
  export_xlsx(res, path = "output/")

  # Single-file mode -- one workbook with sheets res1, res2, res3
  export_xlsx(res, path = "output/all.xlsx")

List names must be non-empty and unique. Each element must be a data.frame, data.table, or tibble. Elements of different types may be mixed freely within the same list.

export_xlsx(
  data,
  path,
  file_col = "excel_name",
  sheet_col = "sheet_name",
  sheet_name = "Sheet1",
  drop_cols = TRUE,
  overwrite = TRUE,
  verbose = FALSE
)

Arguments

data

A data.frame / data.table / tibble, or a named list of such objects. For list input, names become file names (directory mode) or sheet names (single-file mode). Tracking columns (file_col / sheet_col) are optional for data.frame input.

path

character(1). Output destination. A path ending in .xlsx (case-insensitive) is treated as a single workbook; anything else is treated as an output directory. Parent directories are created recursively as needed.

file_col

character(1). Column identifying the source file (used for data.frame input only). Default "excel_name".

sheet_col

character(1). Column identifying the source sheet (used for data.frame input only). Default "sheet_name".

sheet_name

character(1). Worksheet tab name for the single-sheet fallback (plain data.frame with no tracking columns). Default "Sheet1".

drop_cols

logical(1). When TRUE (default) the tracking columns are stripped from every exported sheet.

overwrite

logical(1). Allow overwriting existing files. Default TRUE.

verbose

logical(1). Print a message for every sheet/file written. Default FALSE.

Value

Invisibly, a named character vector of written file paths. In directory mode the vector is named by list-element / file_col value; in single-workbook mode it is named by path.

Details

Why writexl?

writexl writes .xlsx via a minimal C library with no Java or Perl dependency. It is faster than openxlsx for plain export and produces smaller files, at the cost of no cell formatting, formulas, or styles. For those, use openxlsx / openxlsx2.

Sheet-name sanitisation

Excel sheet names are limited to 31 characters and may not contain [ ] * ? / \ :. Both constraints are enforced automatically.

Directory vs. file dispatch

The single path argument is classified purely by its extension: a trailing .xlsx means "one workbook", anything else means "a directory of files". If you want a directory whose name happens to end in .xlsx, append a trailing slash.

Examples

# Example 1: Export a plain data.frame to a single workbook
out_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "test.xlsx")
export_xlsx(
  mtcars,                             # Data to export (no tracking columns)
  path       = out_file,              # Ends in .xlsx -> one workbook
  sheet_name = "mtcars"              # Worksheet tab name for the single sheet
)
file.remove(out_file)
#> [1] TRUE

# Example 2: Export a data.table to a single workbook
# data.table input is handled identically to data.frame
out_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "test.xlsx")
hah <- data.table::as.data.table(mtcars)
export_xlsx(
  hah,                                # data.table input
  path       = out_file,              # Ends in .xlsx -> one workbook
  sheet_name = "mtcars"              # Worksheet tab name for the single sheet
)
file.remove(out_file)
#> [1] TRUE

# Example 3: Split a data.frame into one sheet per group (single workbook)
# `iris` has a `Species` column; each species becomes a separate sheet tab
out_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "iris_by_species.xlsx")
export_xlsx(
  iris,                               # Data to export
  path      = out_file,              # Ends in .xlsx -> one workbook
  file_col  = "Species",             # Column whose unique values name each sheet
  drop_cols = FALSE                  # Keep the Species column in each sheet
)
file.remove(out_file)
#> [1] TRUE

# Example 4: Split a data.table into one sheet per group (single workbook)
out_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "iris_by_species.xlsx")
haha <- data.table::as.data.table(iris)
export_xlsx(
  haha,                              # data.table input
  path      = out_file,              # Ends in .xlsx -> one workbook
  file_col  = "Species",             # Column whose unique values name each sheet
  drop_cols = FALSE                  # Keep the Species column in each sheet
)
file.remove(out_file)
#> [1] TRUE

# Example 5: Split into one file per group (directory mode)
# Omit the .xlsx extension -> directory mode: one file per Species value
out_files <- export_xlsx(
  iris,                              # Data to export
  path      = tempdir(),             # A directory -> one file per file_col value
  file_col  = "Species",            # Column whose values name the output files
  drop_cols = FALSE                 # Keep the Species column in each output file
)
file.remove(out_files)
#> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE

# Example 6: Named list -> single workbook, one sheet per element
# List names (res1, res2) become the sheet tab names
out_file <- file.path(tempdir(), "combined.xlsx")
res <- list(res1 = iris, res2 = mtcars)
export_xlsx(
  res,                               # Named list of data.frames
  path    = out_file                 # Ends in .xlsx -> one workbook
)
file.remove(out_file)
#> [1] TRUE

# Example 7: Named list -> directory, one file per element
# Omit the .xlsx extension -> one file per list element
res <- list(res1 = iris, res2 = mtcars)
out_files <- export_xlsx(
  res,                               # Named list of data.frames
  path    = tempdir()               # A directory -> res1.xlsx, res2.xlsx
)
file.remove(out_files)
#> [1] TRUE TRUE
